questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated block due to fixed partition/paging
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in non-contiguous blocks
Example internal: process needs 10KB but gets 16KB block in fixed partitioning
Example external: after many allocations/deallocations, free memory exists as small scattered holes

questions.question: What is demand paging? Explain how it differs from prepaging.
keyPoints: Demand paging loads pages only when referenced (on page fault)
Initially no pages loaded, pages brought in as needed
Prepaging loads multiple pages in advance anticipating future needs
Demand paging avoids loading unused pages but may cause initial page faults

questions.question: Describe the working set model for controlling thrashing.
keyPoints: Working set: set of pages process currently needs in memory
Defined by window size (recent time period)
If total working set size exceeds available frames, thrashing occurs
OS maintains working set to ensure enough frames per process

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) and how it improves performance.
keyPoints: TLB is hardware cache for page table entries
Stores recently used virtual-to-physical address translations
Associative memory - searches all entries in parallel
On TLB hit: single memory access; on miss: page table walk + memory access
Reduces effective memory access time significantly

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of paging?
keyPoints: Advantages: eliminates external fragmentation, provides simple memory protection, enables sharing
Disadvantages: internal fragmentation exists, complex hardware/software support, page table overhead
Translation overhead (address computation), possible TLB misses

questions.question: Explain the Copy-on-Write technique used in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Parent and child processes initially share same memory pages
Pages marked as read-only
When either process writes to a page, a copy is made
Saves memory and CPU time when child doesn't modify memory
Used in fork() system call implementations

questions.question: What is the purpose of the dirty (modified) bit in a page table entry?
keyPoints: Indicates whether page has been modified since loaded into memory
If set, page must be written back to disk before eviction
If clear, page can be simply discarded (not modified)
Helps optimize I/O operations during page replacement

questions.question: Discuss the FIFO, LRU, and Optimal page replacement algorithms. Explain why FIFO suffers from Belady's Anomaly and why LRU is difficult to implement.
keyPoints: FIFO: queue-based, simple but ignores page usage pattern
LRU: replaces least recently used, better but requires hardware/software support for tracking
Optimal: replaces page not used for longest time future, theoretical benchmark
Belady's Anomaly: FIFO can have MORE faults with MORE frames due to ignoring recency
LRU implementation difficulty: needs hardware support for access tracking, expensive to maintain history stack

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory. Discuss demand paging in detail including the steps involved in handling a page fault.
keyPoints: Virtual memory: illusion of larger memory using disk as extension
Allows programs larger than RAM to execute, provides process isolation
Demand paging: pages loaded only on reference
Page fault handling steps: 1) Check TLB 2) Check page table 3) If invalid, trap to OS 4) Find free frame 5) Read page from disk 6) Update page table 7) Restart instruction
May involve page replacement if no free frames

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain its causes, symptoms, and the techniques operating systems use to prevent or recover from thrashing.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity, CPU spends more time swapping than executing
Causes: overcommitment of memory, too many processes, insufficient frames per process
Symptoms: high page fault rate, low CPU utilization, disk I/O thrashing
Prevention techniques: working set model, page fault frequency, controlling degree of multiprogramming, allocating minimum frames

questions.question: Compare paging and segmentation. Discuss how they can be combined to get advantages of both approaches.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size blocks, eliminates external fragmentation, simple allocation
Segmentation: variable-sized segments based on logical divisions, provides protection and sharing by segment
Problems with pure paging: no logical meaning to pages, internal fragmentation
Problems with pure segmentation: external fragmentation, complex allocation
Combined approach: segment divided into pages (segmented paging) or page table entry includes segment number
Gets logical view of segmentation + efficient memory management of paging
